todaysPlan.reason: Reina has 100% mastery on single-digit facts (16/16 across 4 sessions) but multi-digit with regrouping has never been tested. This is her self-identified math gap and builds toward multiplication readiness. Use competitive framing: 'Can you beat your time on these 3 problems?'
todaysPlan.method: Rapid-fire visual problems with timer: 47+28, 83-56, 124+89. Celebrate with やった after each correct answer.

todaysPlan.reason: Reina has 100% fluency on grade-level narratives (2 sessions) but comprehension depth is literal only ('she went boom, but she was safe'). Before advancing to Constitution reading (parent goal) or Japanese texts, probe inferential thinking with 'Why?' and 'What if?' questions. She requested stories with 'bad guys'—match her interest.
todaysPlan.method: Present 1 action/adventure passage (ninja, spy, or heist theme). After reading, ask: 'Why did the character do that?' 'What would happen if they didn't escape?' 'How did they outsmart the enemy?' Connect to her chess/wrestling strategy brain.

todaysPlan.reason: Homework cycle ingested 4/28: faster/fastest, slower/slowest, colder/coldest. Reina has 100% classroom baseline on spelling tests and mastered 4/4 inflectional endings in session (moving, movers, move, add). This aligns with her pattern-based strength. Deliver in 5-word burst then pivot to her choice.
todaysPlan.method: Competitive framing: 'Can you spell these 5 homework words faster than last time?' Use words from hw-spelling_test-2da310ad cycle. Time her. Celebrate streak with すごい. Stop at 5 words maximum.

todaysPlan.reason: Reina stated subtraction, multiplication, division as her math gaps (profile, 3/27 notes). Never tested across 20+ sessions despite advanced addition/subtraction fluency. Ready for multiplicative reasoning via wrestling metaphors: '3 wrestlers each score 2 points—how many total?' This is her self-identified growth zone.
todaysPlan.method: Present 2-3 simple multiplication scenarios using wrestling context. Start with repeated addition visual (2+2+2 = 3 groups of 2). Ask her to solve, then introduce multiplication symbol. No drill—just concept building.
skipConditions: If math warm-up took longer than 5 minutes
If she requests different activity after reading

todaysPlan.reason: Advanced vocabulary claimed in profile but never formally assessed across 20+ sessions. Required baseline before Japanese kanji/katakana work (parent goal: June 2026 Japan trip). Reading sessions show comprehension but definitional depth unknown. Sample 5-7 words from grade 3-4 level.
todaysPlan.method: Ask: 'What does [word] mean?' For words like: champion, dangerous, escape, ancient, brave. Accept explanations, synonyms, or usage examples. Note depth of response (surface vs. elaborated definitions).
skipConditions: If session time is running low
If she is fatigued from prior activities

todaysPlan.activity: Student Choice (Chess, Japanese, or Strategy Game)
todaysPlan.reason: Reina self-regulates well and requests specific activities (riddles, Space Invaders, reading). Honoring her choice builds agency and maintains engagement. She listed chess, piano, Japanese kanji as goals. Offer her the menu.
todaysPlan.method: Ask: 'You crushed today's work. What do you want to tackle next—chess strategy, Japanese vocabulary, or something else?' Follow her lead. If she chooses game, allow it as earned reward.
skipConditions: If she exits session before this activity
If prior required activities took full session time

childProfile: Reina is an 8-year-old academically advanced learner who thrives on challenge, competition, and speed. She is a year ahead of grade level, scores 100% on spelling tests, and has mastered single-digit math facts with zero errors across multiple sessions. She is bilingual (English + Japanese) and proud of it—celebrate her wins with すごい and やった. Reina is a competitive wrestler who responds to wrestling metaphors (takedowns, pins, championships) and scoreboard framing ('Can you beat your record?'). She loves strategy (chess, riddles, problem-solving) and engages deeply when material is hard, not when it's easy. She disengages from rote drill (worksheet coin counting crashed to 26% accuracy) but masters applied contexts (store-game 86%, word problems 100%). She self-advocates clearly ('Stop. Thank you' when done, 'I need an easier hint' when stuck) and corrects the tutor when errors occur. She loves the movie Matilda, action/adventure stories with 'bad guys,' and wants to learn kanji/katakana before her Japan trip (June 2026). Match her fast pace, push her hard, acknowledge her intelligence, and pivot when she requests it—she knows what she needs.
stopAfter: Stop when Reina self-regulates an exit ('I have to go,' 'I'm done') or after completing 3-4 required activities with high accuracy (90%+). If she requests a game or different activity mid-session, honor it—she has earned autonomy. Do not force completion of all 6 activities; her engagement quality matters more than activity count. End on a win (correct answer, personal best, or mastered concept) so she leaves confident.
rewardPolicy: Reina earns games (Space Invaders, store-game) after demonstrating effort and accuracy on challenging work, not as bribery to start. She is smart enough to see through 'do this, then play'—instead, frame games as part of the learning (store-game taught coin counting better than worksheets). If she requests a game mid-session, allow brief access (2-3 minutes) then return to work. She self-regulates well and will re-engage if the next activity is appropriately challenging. Celebrate streaks and personal bests with Japanese phrases (すごい, やった) and wrestling metaphors ('That's a pin!'). Do not over-praise easy tasks—she wants acknowledgment of real achievement.
